meta.title: OpenTechEvents — an open spec for tech community events
meta.description: An open specification to describe, publish and share tech community events. Publish once, be everywhere.

draft.body: The spec is not stable yet: fields can still change, be renamed or be dropped. The core of v0.1 is being decided right now.

hero.title: Publish your event once. Let it show up everywhere.
hero.lead: OpenTechEvents (OTE) is an open, free specification to describe tech community events — meetups, conferences, workshops, online or in person — in a single format that directories, calendars and tools can read automatically.
hero.note: No platform to sign up for. No tool to install. It's just a file you publish.

problem.title: You already know the problem
problem.lead: You organise one event. Then you announce it five times, by hand, in five different formats — and you still miss half the people who would have come.

c1.title: The same event, typed again and again
c1.body: Meetup, Eventbrite, LinkedIn, your own website, a community directory, three WhatsApp groups. Same data, six forms, six chances to make a typo.

c2.title: Directories can't find you
c2.body: Event directories want to list you. But they'd have to scrape your site or wait for you to open a pull request. So most of the time, they just don't.

c3.title: Every change means doing it all over
c3.body: Venue moved. Speaker cancelled. New date. Now go update every single platform — or live with the stale copies out there.

problem.pull: There is no shortage of information about your events. What's missing is interoperability.

spec.title: One file. That's the whole idea.
spec.lead: You describe your events in a simple JSON file and publish it at a stable URL. Anything that speaks OTE — directories, calendars, bots, websites — can then read it without asking you for anything.

s1.title: You publish
s1.body: A feed with your events at a URL you control, e.g. yourcommunity.dev/events.json.

s2.title: Tools read it
s2.body: Aggregators, directories and converters pick it up automatically — and turn it into RSS, iCalendar or schema.org.

s3.title: People find you
s3.body: Attendees subscribe by topic or city and get your events in the tools they already use — no extra platform.

min.title: The minimum is genuinely minimal
min.body: A valid event needs little more than a name, a start date, a time zone and where it happens. Optional modules — CFP, speakers, price, accessibility — cover the rest when you need them.
min.caveat: Careful, because we'd rather be straight with you: that draft — the core fields and the optional modules alike — has not been agreed yet. It's there to show the shape of the thing and to open the debate, and it will change. What we're asking you to adopt today is the idea, and to come and break it before it sets.
min.b1: Plain JSON. No SDK, no account, no server required — a static file is enough.
min.b2: Designed to map cleanly onto RSS, iCalendar and schema.org/Event.
min.b3: Works the same for a recurring meetup, a multi-day conference or an online workshop.
min.b4: Open licence, no usage restrictions — in the spirit of RSS and iCal.
min.caption: Minimal valid event

docs.title: Read the full specification
docs.lead: Everything is public and in the open — including what's still undecided. The spec is at version 0.x: expect it to change, and expect your feedback to be what changes it.

d1.title: Data model
d1.body: Every field of an event: what's required, what's optional, and how it maps to iCalendar, RSS and schema.org.

d2.title: Feed format
d2.body: How to group several events into a feed: metadata, pagination, licence and discovery.

d3.title: Examples
d3.body: Complete documents to copy: a minimal event, a meetup, a conference with an open CFP, and a full feed.

d4.title: Research
d4.body: The analysis behind the decisions: existing standards, platforms and directories we studied.

contrib.title: Something doesn't fit your community?
contrib.body: Then the spec is wrong, not your community. It's still a draft precisely so that organisers can push back before anything is set in stone — a missing field, a format that breaks your case, a name that makes no sense. Say so in an issue.

benefits.title: What you get out of it
benefits.lead: Adopting OTE is a couple of hours of work, once. Here's what it buys you.

b1.title: Stop doing data entry
b1.body: Write the event once. Let tooling push it to directories, calendars and feeds instead of you copy-pasting it into yet another form.

b2.title: Reach beyond your own bubble
b2.body: Directories and aggregators can pick up your events the moment you publish them. Your meetup shows up where people are actually looking.

b3.title: Own your data
b3.body: Your events live at your URL, in an open format. If a platform shuts down, changes its API or its pricing, your history and your feed are still yours.

b4.title: Free integrations, forever
b4.body: Every new tool built on OTE — an .ics export, a website widget, a Telegram bot — works for you without you lifting a finger.

b5.title: Accurate everywhere
b5.body: Change the date in one place and every consumer that re-reads your feed gets the correction. No more zombie listings with the old venue.

b6.title: Shape the standard
b6.body: The spec is in draft. Early adopters decide what it looks like — if something doesn't fit your community, it can still be changed.

adopt.title: How to adopt it
adopt.lead: Three steps. The first one is the only one that takes real work, and it's a text file.

s1.title: Publish an OTE feed
s1.body: Create a JSON file with your events and host it anywhere you already publish — your site, GitHub Pages, a gist. If your events live in a Google Calendar or Meetup, a converter can generate the feed for you.

s2.title: Make it discoverable
s2.body: Add one line to the <head> of your website so tools can find the feed on their own — the same way RSS readers do.
s2.caption: In your site's <head>

s3.title: Tell us, and get listed
s3.body: Open an issue with your feed URL. We'll validate it, list your community on this page, and aggregators will start picking up your events.

ics.tag: Already have an .ics? Skip all of the above.
ics.title: Give us your calendar and we'll do the conversion
ics.body: If your events already live in a public calendar (Google Calendar, Nextcloud, your own site), you don't have to write a single line of JSON to try this out. Register the .ics URL as a source in the OTE aggregator: it reads your calendar, converts it to OTE and republishes it as a feed — including .ics, RSS and JSON Feed exports.
ics.body2: You commit to nothing and you change nothing. It's the cheapest way to see what your events look like in OTE — and if it's not worth it, you walk away having lost one issue.
ics.caveat: Honest caveat: the aggregator is being built right now, and the MVP starts precisely with .ics. Registering your calendar today means being one of the first sources it ingests — and its first real test.

help.title: Not comfortable with JSON?
help.body: That's fine — open an issue anyway and tell us where your events live today. Helping the first communities get on board is exactly what this stage of the project is for.

adopters.title: Who publishes in OTE
adopters.lead: Communities and events already exposing their data in this format.
adopters.empty: The spec is brand new, so this list is still short. Be one of the first — early adopters get a say in what the standard becomes.

consumers.title: Who consumes OTE data
consumers.lead: Directories, aggregators and people reusing feeds published in this format. Every one of them is a place your events can reach for free.
consumers.empty: Building a directory, a newsletter or a bot? Consume OTE feeds and get listed here.

tools.title: Ecosystem of tools
tools.lead: What exists today, and what's up for grabs. Claim one by opening an issue.

items.q: Does this compete with Meetup, Eventbrite or Luma?
items.a: No. OTE is a data format, not a platform. The point is to interoperate with those tools: describe the event once, then publish or convert it towards whatever platforms you already use.

items.q: Do I have to stop using my current tools?
items.a: No — quite the opposite. The goal is that your data flows into the platforms and tools you already work with, instead of you retyping it into each one.

items.q: Does it replace RSS or iCalendar?
items.a: No. OTE is designed to be convertible to them. An OTE feed can be exported to RSS, JSON Feed or iCal so attendees consume it in their feed reader or calendar app.

items.q: How much work is this really?
items.a: For a small community: writing one JSON file and adding a link tag to your site. If you already generate your events page from data, it's usually a matter of emitting one more file.
Not yet. We're at version 0.x and everything — name, fields, licence, governance — is open to debate. That's precisely why joining now is worth it: early adopters shape the format.

The proposal comes from Community Builders (ComBuildersES) and is now developed in its own home, the OpenTechEvents organisation on GitHub, with an international scope. It is not an official standard yet — it's an open, community-driven design effort, and it needs organisers like you to become one.

final.title: A standard is worth exactly as much as the number of people using it
final.body: Every community that publishes an OTE feed makes the format more useful for every other community — and for everyone trying to find your events.

footer.note: An initiative by Community Builders. Draft specification — the name, scope and licence are still provisional.
